findings.description: If sendmail (line 60) or the SMTP() constructor (line 58) raises on a transient/recipient error, the exception propagates out of flush so the loop aborts, remaining messages are never sent, and _pending.clear() (line 65) never runs — leaving already-sent messages in the queue so a retry re-delivers them (duplicate emails).

Dedup key is msg.to (recipient only), so two genuinely different messages (different subject/body/template) to the same address cause the second to be silently skipped and counted as skipped — distinct emails are lost; the key should be content-based, not recipient-only.

flush clears _pending but never _seen, so an address stays deduplicated for the queue's entire lifetime (until manual clear_dedup_cache): the same recipient can never be enqueued again after a flush, and _seen grows unbounded (memory leak) in a long-running pipeline — contradicting the docstring's 'already queued' scope.

Out-of-scope passing note (defer to critic-security): _render interpolates unsanitized msg.to and msg.subject into CRLF-delimited headers, enabling SMTP header/body injection (e.g. injected Bcc) if those fields contain \r\n.

Out-of-scope passing note (defer to critic-performance): a fresh SMTP connection is opened and quit() per message inside the loop, so each message pays a full TCP+SMTP handshake — the connection should be reused across the batch.
